beats.text: Plot any liquid asset's daily returns and the wild days arrive in bunches, separated by long stretches of calm.
Mandelbrot noted it in 1963: large changes tend to be followed by large changes, and small changes by small ones.
Classical models assume homoscedasticity, a constant noise variance, which treats a calm Tuesday and a panicked Thursday alike.
Raw returns are serially uncorrelated, yet squared returns are strongly positively autocorrelated across many lags.

visual.note: The level is noise; the magnitude has memory

beats.text: Returns are also fat-tailed, with far more four and five sigma days than a Gaussian distribution would allow.

visual.label: sigma days a Gaussian rules out but markets show

beats.text: GARCH does not predict returns; it predicts the scale of returns, the width of tomorrow's distribution.

visual.sub: Not the center of the distribution

beats.text: Robert Engle's 1982 insight, which won the 2003 Nobel, let the variance of the current shock depend on recent shock magnitudes.
Write the return as a standardized innovation scaled by a time-varying volatility built from past squared shocks.

visual.note: All structure lives in the conditional variance

beats.text: A large move yesterday mechanically inflates today's variance, then feeds forward, which is clustering written as a recursion.
For positive, stationary variance the constraints are omega above zero, the alphas non-negative, and their sum below one.
ARCH is clumsy: persistence over weeks forces a large q of five to ten lags and a long parameter vector prone to overfitting.
Parameters are fit by maximum likelihood, rewarding a variance that is large on violent days and small on calm ones.
The Generalized ARCH model adds one term, yesterday's variance, and that single addition has made it the default for forty years.
Three forces set tomorrow's variance: a constant floor, the reaction to yesterday's shock, and carried-over memory.

visual.note: Floor, news, memory in three parameters

beats.text: Persistence is the sum alpha plus beta, and for daily equity indices it sits famously close to one, typically 0.95 to 0.99.
Variance always pulls back toward its long-run level, the recursion's mean, given by omega over one minus persistence.

visual.note: Mean reversion toward the long-run variance

beats.text: Worked example: omega 0.00001, alpha 0.1 times shock 0.0009, plus beta 0.85 times variance 0.0004, gives 0.00044.
That variance maps to a daily volatility near 2.1 percent, and the closer persistence is to one, the slower the decay.
Plain GARCH reacts to the squared shock, so squaring throws away the sign and a minus four and plus four percent day look identical.

visual.sub: Bad news raises volatility more than good

beats.text: The leverage effect means a falling stock raises its debt-to-equity ratio and triggers forced selling, mechanically raising risk.
GJR-GARCH is the minimal fix: add one term that switches on only after a negative shock, with a positive gamma.

visual.note: Gamma is the leverage effect as a parameter

beats.text: EGARCH models the log of variance, guaranteeing positivity without sign constraints and letting the sign of the shock matter.

visual.note: Theta below zero encodes the leverage tilt

beats.text: The news-impact curve makes it visible: GARCH is a symmetric parabola, while GJR and EGARCH steepen the bad-news arm.
Hansen and Lunde's 2005 horse race found nothing reliably beat a plain GARCH(1,1) for daily equity forecasting.

visual.label: horse race: GARCH(1,1) hard to beat

beats.text: The payoff of a fitted GARCH model is a forecast of future variance, and the recursion makes multi-step forecasts almost free.

visual.sub: From conditional variance to risk numbers

beats.text: Beyond one step the unknown future shock is replaced by its expectation, collapsing to clean geometric mean reversion.
From a panic the forecast decays toward the long-run level; from a calm start it climbs toward it.
Value-at-Risk plugs the conditional volatility into the quantile of the innovation distribution to bound the loss.
Because sigma is conditional, the VaR breathes with the market, widening in turbulence and tightening in calm.
GARCH is not the last word; realized-volatility, stochastic-volatility, and implied-volatility models all challenge it.
